htmlContent:
The moment someone says "calm down" when you're already drowning

You know the feeling. Your heart is pounding. Your chest is tight. Your thoughts are racing in a loop that won't stop. And then someone looks at you and says it. Those two words. "Calm down."

And something inside you explodes. Or shuts down. Or both, somehow, at the same time.

I've been there. More times than I can count. And for years I thought there was something wrong with me. Why couldn't I just take a breath and relax? Why did those words feel like gasoline on a fire?

Here's the thing. That reaction isn't a character flaw. It's not you being difficult or dramatic. It's your nervous system doing exactly what it evolved to do.

Let me explain what's actually happening when someone tells you to calm down. Because understanding this changed everything for me. And I think it might for you too.

The neuroscience of being told to calm down

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping out how our nervous system responds to safety and danger. His work shows that we have three primary states: ventral vagal (safe and social), sympathetic (fight or flight), and dorsal vagal (freeze or collapse).

When you're activated, you're in sympathetic or dorsal vagal state. Your body believes there's a threat. Maybe it's a real threat. Maybe it's a memory. Maybe it's a feeling that reminds your body of something that happened years ago. Your nervous system doesn't distinguish between the two. It just knows danger is here.

Now someone says "calm down." What does your nervous system hear? It hears "the person who's supposed to be safe doesn't see my danger." It hears "I'm alone in this." It hears "my experience is wrong."

That's not a rational thought. It's a body-level response. Your amygdala fires. Your cortisol spikes. Your heart rate increases even more. You're now more activated than you were before.

Wild, right? The instruction to calm down makes you less calm. Every single time.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote The Body Keeps the Score, talks about how trauma survivors often have a mismatch between what their body is feeling and what the environment actually requires. When someone tells you to calm down, it reinforces that mismatch. It says "your body is lying to you." And that feels like betrayal.

Because your body isn't lying. Your body is telling the truth about what it's been through. The problem isn't your reaction. The problem is that no one is seeing the fire you're trying to put out.

Why "calm down" lands as invalidation

Think about the last time you were really upset. Not mildly annoyed. I mean the kind of upset where your hands are shaking and you can't find words. The kind where you feel like you're going to fly out of your skin or sink through the floor.

Someone tells you to calm down. What did you feel? For me, it was rage. Then shame. Then more rage. Then silence because I didn't trust myself to speak.

Here's what's happening under the surface. The phrase "calm down" carries a hidden message. It says "your emotional state is a problem for me." It says "I need you to be different right now so I can feel comfortable." It says "you're too much."

And if you grew up with caregivers who couldn't handle your big feelings, that message hits an old wound. A very deep one. One that says "you have to shrink yourself to be loved."

Richard Schwartz, the founder of Internal Family Systems, would say that this activates what he calls "exile" parts. The young parts of you that learned to hide their feelings because showing them was dangerous. When someone tells you to calm down, those parts wake up. They say "see? It's happening again. We're not safe. We're too much."

And then you're not just dealing with the original activation. You're dealing with the activation of being told to calm down on top of it. Layer upon layer. Like stacking weights on a chest that's already struggling to breathe.

I want you to notice something. Notice what just happened in your body when I described that. Did your shoulders tighten? Did your jaw clench? Did your breath get shallow? That's your nervous system recognizing a pattern. That's the memory living in your tissue.

Sit with that. Just for a moment. Don't try to change it. Don't try to fix it. Just notice.

That noticing is the first step toward something different. Because you can't regulate what you can't see.

What actually helps when someone tells you to calm down

Okay so now we know why it hurts. Let's talk about what to do with it. Because you can't control other people. Trust me, I've tried. But you can build a different relationship with your own nervous system.

First, recognize that the person telling you to calm down is probably also dysregulated. They're uncomfortable with your activation. Their own nervous system is trying to get back to safety by asking you to change. It's not right. But it helps to understand it. It helps to see that their words are about their discomfort, not about your validity.

Second, give yourself permission to not calm down. I know that sounds backwards. Stay with me here. When you fight the activation, you make it worse. When you say "I shouldn't be feeling this," your nervous system hears "the feeling is dangerous" and turns up the volume. But when you say "okay, I'm activated. This is what activation feels like in my body," something shifts. Not immediately. Not completely. But something shifts.

Peter Levine, the creator of Somatic Experiencing, talks about pendulation. Moving between activation and settling, slowly, gently, without forcing. The goal isn't to get to calm. The goal is to widen your window of tolerance so you can be with the activation without it taking over.

For those looking for gentle nervous system support, something worth trying is NOW L-Theanine 200mg (paid link). It's an amino acid that can help take the edge off without making you feel sedated. I use it sometimes when I feel that spike coming and need a little buffer. It's not a cure. But it can help create enough space to actually use the skills you're learning.

Third, practice the pause. When someone says "calm down," your instinct will be to react. Fight or flight. Say something sharp. Shut down completely. But if you can create even a two-second pause, you give your prefrontal cortex a chance to come back online. Take a breath. Count to three in your head. Say nothing if you need to. The pause is not compliance. The pause is strategy.

A breathing tool that helps slow the exhale for nervous system regulation is Breathing Exercise Tool (paid link). It forces you to extend your exhale, which directly activates the vagus nerve. I keep one in my bag. When I feel that "calm down" rage rising, I use it. Not to calm down for them. To calm down for me.

Building a new relationship with activation

Here's what I want you to take away from this. Your reaction to being told to calm down is not a sign that you're broken. It's a sign that your nervous system is working exactly as it should. It's trying to protect you. It's trying to keep you alive. The problem isn't your system. The problem is that no one taught you how to work with it.

Deb Dana, who writes about Polyvagal Theory in practice, talks about "glimmers." Small moments of safety that we can train ourselves to notice. A warm cup of tea. A soft blanket. The sound of rain. These aren't fixes. But they're anchors. They help your nervous system remember that safety exists, even in the middle of activation.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure stimulation can help your body feel held when your mind is spinning. I use mine when I'm coming down from a big activation. It doesn't erase what happened. But it helps my body know it's safe now.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work on structural dissociation helped me understand why different parts of me react so differently to the same trigger. One part wants to fight. One part wants to hide. Neither is wrong. They're both trying to help.

The next time someone tells you to calm down, I want you to try something. Instead of fighting it, instead of collapsing into it, just say to yourself: "I hear you. I'm not going to calm down right now. But I'm going to stay with myself while I'm activated."

That's it. That's the practice. Not calm. Presence. Not fixing. Being with.

It won't feel good at first. It might feel terrible. But over time, something shifts. Your nervous system starts to trust that you'll stay. That you won't abandon yourself when things get hard. And that trust is the foundation of real regulation.

You don't need to calm down. You need to be seen. Start with seeing yourself.
